one_line_takeaway: UnPII scales existing gradient-based LLM unlearning losses (GA/NPO/DPO) by a composite, tanh-bounded PII Risk Index so that higher-risk PII attributes receive stronger forgetting signal, yielding up to 11.8% accuracy, 6.3% utility, and 12.4% generalizability gains over unscaled baselines at a ~27.5% average fine-tuning overhead, evaluated on a purpose-built 1,700-sample synthetic PII dataset and LLaMA2-7B.
key_finding: Across GA, NPO, and DPO baselines and three forgetting ratios (1%, 5%, 10%), adding UnPII's risk-weighted gradient scaling improved H-AUG by up to 5% over the respective unmodified baselines. Decomposed by metric, UnPII improved forgetting accuracy by up to 11.8%, utility by up to 6.3%, and generalizability by up to 12.4% relative to baselines (forgetting accuracy gains were roughly 5% at most steps). DPO+UnPII and NPO+UnPII showed up to 8% utility gains, while GA+UnPII showed a more modest ~2% utility gain; generalizability improved by up to ~3%. Fine-tuning overhead over 20 unlearning steps averaged 27.5%, breaking down to GA 465.7s->562.0s (+21.6%), DPO 628.1s->790.1s (+25.3%), NPO 474.4s->642.3s (+35.6%); this overhead is described as modest relative to full retraining of LLaMA2 7B (cited as requiring 184,320 GPU hours). The dominant overhead source is PII-detection API calls to GPT-4o-mini, costing about $0.01 for 320 calls across an entire run. A consistency experiment (original vs. re-sampled forget sets, Section 5.3) found H-AUG remained largely stable across sample compositions. The paper also reports that combinations of five or more PII attributes push PRI values close to 1.0 with low variance across 1,000 simulations (Figure 2), and notes a specific hyperparameter sensitivity finding: lowering lambda from 0.025 to 0.0125 improved a reported score from 86.05 to 89.24 on the GA+forget05 configuration that had shown anomalous degradation.
core_contribution: The paper introduces UnPII, a PII-centric machine unlearning approach that dynamically prioritizes forgetting strength based on a quantifiable privacy-risk score of each PII attribute (or combination of attributes), rather than applying a uniform forgetting strategy to all data. Concretely, UnPII (1) identifies PII present in an LLM's outputs by prompting an external LLM (GPT-4o mini) with PII-inducing queries, (2) computes a PII Risk Index (PRI) -- a bounded (0,1) composite score derived from seven weighted risk factors (identifiability, sensitivity, usability, linkability, permanency, exposability, compliancy) via a tanh-bounded weighted-sum formula with a lambda term (lambda=0.025) to avoid premature saturation, and (3) plugs the PRI into the loss of existing gradient-based unlearning methods as a per-sample scaling factor: L_UnPII = L_base * (1 + R_p), where L_base is Gradient Ascent (GA), Negative Preference Optimization (NPO), or Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). The authors also contribute a synthetic 1,700-sample PII dataset (17 attribute categories: 10 single-type, 7 combined-type, 100 QA pairs each) built with GPT-4o, used to fine-tune a PII-containing LLaMA2-7B model via LoRA, which is then subjected to unlearning.
threat_model: The setting is not adversarial in the classic exploit sense; it is a compliance/privacy-risk model. A model provider has already trained an LLM (LLaMA2 7B) on data containing PII across 12 categories (basic, contact, identifiers, financial, biometric, medical, employment, education, digital footprint, location, legal, misc). Under 'right to be forgotten' regulations (GDPR, CCPA, PIPL), the provider must remove specific individuals' PII from the trained model without access to a retention/reference dataset and without full retraining. The risk being modeled is exposure risk upon PII disclosure through model outputs -- e.g., an attacker or unintended user eliciting memorized PII via prompting -- captured via a composite score covering re-identification uniqueness, harm potential, attacker usefulness, linkability to other data, permanency, exposure frequency, and regulatory severity. There is no adaptive/adversarial attacker modeled against the unlearning procedure itself (e.g., no membership-inference or relearning-attack evaluation of residual leakage); the paper explicitly flags this in its limitations (Section 7).
limitations: Limitations are explicitly stated by the authors in Section 7 (Threats to Validity): (1) the evaluation relies entirely on an in-house synthetic PII dataset generated by GPT-4o, which may not capture real-world data noise, class imbalance, long-tail formats, or locale-specific identifier formats, and generation may itself contain factually inconsistent artifacts (e.g., mismatched city/ZIP); (2) the seven risk factors are derived from NIST/DHS/HIPAA guidance but were not validated by legal or domain experts, and PRI weights in the experiments were assigned by GPT-4o rather than an organizational policy, introducing potential model bias; (3) there is no accepted ground-truth metric for 'true' unlearning -- the paper's own H-AUG metric is an empirical proxy, not a worst-case forgetting guarantee, and PII-detection validation itself depends on a commercial LLM (GPT-4o-mini) introducing measurement variability; (4) UnPII's benefit is uneven across base methods -- the authors hypothesize it synergizes best with GA's simple negative-log-likelihood loss, while DPO/NPO's sigmoid-based intermediary transformations dampen the scaling factor's effect, and an anomalous degradation was observed for GA+UnPII on forget05, traced to sensitivity of the lambda hyperparameter; (5) reducing multidimensional real-world privacy risk to a single bounded (0,1) scalar is acknowledged as a simplification requiring human-in-the-loop auditing before real-world/industrial deployment. No adversarial evaluation (e.g., membership inference or relearning attacks probing residual memorization) is performed.

datasets_used: Synthetic PII dataset constructed by the authors using GPT-4o: 1,700 QA pairs across 17 PII categories (10 single-type + 7 combined-type, 100 samples each), used to fine-tune LLaMA2 7B via LoRA into a 'PII-containing model'
forget01 / forget05 / forget10 splits: 17 / 85 / 170 samples (1% / 5% / 10% of the 1,700-sample set)

Enron Email Dataset consulted only to derive statistical distributions (name frequency, length, character n-grams) for realistic synthetic name generation, per the Ethics Consideration section; no real identifiers were reused
quantitative_results: H-AUG improvement up to 5% over baselines across GA/NPO/DPO and forget01/05/10 settings (Figure 3)
Forgetting accuracy improvement up to 11.8% with UnPII, roughly 5% at most steps
Utility improvement up to 6.3% overall; DPO+UnPII and NPO+UnPII up to 8%, GA+UnPII around 2%
Generalizability improvement up to 12.4% overall (Figure 4 caption); ~3% improvement described in text discussion (Section 5.2) -- note the paper states both figures in different places
Fine-tuning overhead: average 27.5%; GA 465.7s->562.0s (+21.6%), DPO 628.1s->790.1s (+25.3%), NPO 474.4s->642.3s (+35.6%), measured over 20 unlearning steps (Table 3)
PII-detection cost via GPT-4o-mini API: ~$0.01 for 320 calls across an entire run
Original LLaMA2 7B full retraining cited as requiring 184,320 GPU hours, used as a comparison point for the overhead's modesty
PRI simulation: with 5+ exposed PII attributes, PRI approaches 1.0 with low standard deviation across 1,000 simulations (Figure 2)
Hyperparameter sensitivity: lowering lambda in the PRI formula from 0.025 to 0.0125 improved a score from 86.05 to 89.24 for the anomalous GA+UnPII forget05 configuration

Zip code + birth date + gender combination cited (from prior literature, not this paper's own result) as uniquely identifying 87% of Americans -- this is a cited statistic, not an experimental finding of this paper
baselines_compared: Gradient Ascent (GA) -- Jang et al. 2023, cited as [23]
Negative Preference Optimization (NPO) -- Zhang et al., cited as [47]
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) -- Rafailov et al., cited as [34], adapted here with 'I don't know' as the preferred response
